Party_Details.Name: Han Cheng Fong

Facts.Text: Han Cheng Fong is a former Chief Executive Officer of Fraser & Neave Ltd and a former Deputy Chairman of DBS Land Ltd with experience in the property market in Singapore and China.

In early 2010, the founder directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd invited Han Cheng Fong to participate in a low-carbon real estate project in Hangzhou, China, known as the Hangzhou-Singapore Eco-Park.

On 23 February 2010, an email from Lim Shih Hsi Richard to Christine Liew and others recorded that Christine had proposed a formal agreement among Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd shareholders to govern their business relationship and that a first draft would be prepared soon.

On 28 February 2010, an email from Lim Shih Hsi Richard stated that Christine requested preparation of a simple shareholders' agreement among the six directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd to be signed by 1 March 2010 and that a resolution would be sent out by 1 March 2010 covering shareholdings, appointment of Han and Christine as directors, Han as Deputy Chairman of CTP and its subsidiaries, and proposed profit sharing.

On 1 March 2010, a document titled 'Directors' Resolution in Writing Pursuant to the Company's Articles of Association' was signed by Patrick Teo, Han Cheng Fong, Richard SH Lim, Robin Low, Michael Heng, Christine Liew and on behalf of Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd, recording that with effect from 1 March 2010 each of the six individuals would hold two ordinary shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd would hold four ordinary shares, for a total of 16 shares.

The 1 March 2010 document recorded that Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd would set up CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd as a 100% subsidiary to be the special purpose vehicle for the Hangzhou-Singapore Eco-Park project, that the CTP-Hangzhou board would consist of all six directors of CTP with Han Cheng Fong as Chairman and Christine Liew as CEO, that net income would be split 67% to CTP-Hangzhou and 33% to CTP, and that of CTP-Hangzhou's 67% share, 33% would be distributed to its board and 67% to CTP-Hangzhou management headed by Han and Christine at Han's sole discretion.
The 1 March 2010 document stated that any change to the recorded resolutions required a unanimous decision of the board of directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.
Under the 1 March 2010 document, Han Cheng Fong was designated as Deputy Chairman of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and as Chairman of the board of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd to be formed.

On 23 March 2010, Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd entered into a tripartite agreement with Hangzhou Qianjiang Economic Development Area Management Committee and Hangzhou Vanwarm Holdings Group Ltd to collaborate on preparation of a master plan, development and promotion of the Hangzhou-Singapore Eco-Park project.

On 24 March 2010, the Business Times published a report stating that Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd was exploring potential deals including a coal gasification project in Indonesia, a wind farm project in Vietnam and a solar panel installation business in China, based on an interview with Patrick Teo, Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Michael Heng.

On 7 April 2010, Han informed the directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd by email that he would focus on CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and not sit on the CTP board so as not to stand in the way of CTP's fund-raising efforts, and the CTP directors accepted this position.

On 1 April 2010, CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd was incorporated as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, and its directors were recorded as Han Cheng Fong as chairman, together with Robin Low, Patrick Teo, Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Christine Liew.

On 31 May 2010, Han signed a collaboration agreement on behalf of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd with Hangzhou Vanwarm Holdings Group Ltd to set up a joint venture company named Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd, under which CTP-Hangzhou was to receive a guaranteed profit of RMB130 million from the Hangzhou project and Vanwarm would arrange for Hong Kong Hong Jia Investments Co Ltd to lend CTP-Hangzhou USD6 million to fund CTP-Hangzhou's 40% share of the joint venture's registered capital of USD15 million.

On 1 June 2010, Han sent an email to Patrick Teo referring to an earlier meeting where Patrick had reprimanded Christine Liew, stating that if he had known of the outburst before signing, he would not have signed and the project would not proceed, and emphasising his efforts to get the project off the ground while managing financial and reputational risks.

On 3 June 2010, an email from Lim Shih Hsi Richard to Patrick Teo discussed a proposal to reduce Han's share to a maximum of 10%, remove him from the chairmanship of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, remove Han and Christine as bank signatories, and to freeze them from further information and updates until they felt useless.

On 3 June 2010, Patrick Teo replied to Lim Shih Hsi Richard stating that achieving the goals regarding Han and Christine would trigger a confrontation, that he preferred an 'elegant' approach via a marathon meeting, that CTP and CTP-Hangzhou were cleantech vendors so Han and Christine's roles could be diminished, and that they could freeze Han and Christine from further information and updates until they felt useless, anticipating that Han might resign.

On 17 June 2010, Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd was incorporated in China, and CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd appointed Han Cheng Fong and Robin Low as its directors alongside Vanwarm representatives Liu Hai, Chen Yang and Zeng Jimin.

By August 2010, Han had obtained emails exchanged between Patrick Teo, Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Robin Low that discussed sidelining or removing Han and Christine from their roles in relation to CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd.

By August 2010, Han became aware that Patrick Teo and Lim Shih Hsi Richard were exploring a sale of their shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd to Bintan Kindenko Corporation Berhad, a Malaysian company, despite an understanding with Hangzhou Qianjiang Economic Development Area Management Committee and Vanwarm that the Hangzhou project was to be a China-Singapore project.

On 17 August 2010, an email from Frankie Tan to Patrick Teo suggested that Han and Christine should be removed before concluding a deal with Bintan Kindenko Corporation Berhad, stating that they might block the deal once announced.

On 17 August 2010, Patrick Teo emailed Frankie Tan describing a plan involving a Malaysian company controlled by Frankie and Ong Puay Koon to subscribe for shares so that Han and Christine's combined shareholding could be diluted to about 19.23% or further, and stating that at the time of the cash subscription they would ask Han to step down from the chair of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and Christine from Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.

On 24 August 2010, Patrick Teo emailed Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Robin Low stating that he was planning something with Frankie Tan to get rid of Han and Christine and that they were going to be ruthless.

On 30 August 2010, Patrick Teo emailed Harpal Mahtani stating that he and his partners planned to exchange their shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd for shares in a public listed company, that an altercation with Han would create problems, and that he needed to get Han out of the way or neutralise him.
On 25 August 2010, Han instructed company secretary Ng Chee Tiong to ask all directors of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd to disclose their directorships in other entities.
On 5 September 2010, Patrick Teo emailed the other CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd directors stating that he would address issues raised by Han in a memo to Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd members at a later date.
On 18 September 2010, Christine Liew and Robin Low incorporated Green Solutions @ ARB Pte Ltd in Singapore, which later changed its name to International Eco-City Pte Ltd on 20 January 2011.
On 24 September 2010, Han arranged for a CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board meeting to be held on 29 September 2010.

On 27 September 2010, after receiving notice of the 29 September 2010 CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board meeting, Patrick Teo emailed Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Robin Low asking them not to attend, stating that attendance could lead to a breakup of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and that Han had planned to catch them off guard.

On 29 September 2010, a CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board meeting attended by Han, Robin Low and Christine Liew resolved to replace company secretary Ng Chee Tiong with Sally Phuar, to change the company's registered address to that of the new company secretary, and to confirm that CTP-Hangzhou had five directors, namely Patrick, Richard, Han, Robin and Christine, with one board seat vacant.

On 1 October 2010, Patrick Teo instructed company secretary Ng Chee Tiong to retain CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd's records until further notice because the majority of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd's board did not agree to the change of company secretary.

On 5 October 2010, Han emailed Patrick Teo stating that he regretted being involved with Patrick and Richard, that he was committed to seeing the Hangzhou project through for the Chinese partners, that he would do what he could to ensure Patrick and Richard stayed within the law and observed good governance, and that he had documentary proof of their conduct over the previous months which he had communicated to Liu Hai and Vanwarm.
On 6 October 2010, Patrick Teo emailed Han stating that he did not have the time or passion to spar with Han and reserving his comments on Han's views on corporate governance.

On 12 October 2010, an Extraordinary General Meeting of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd was held at Patrick Teo's initiative at which Han and Christine were removed as directors of CTP-Hangzhou with immediate effect and Michael Heng was appointed a director; on the same day, a CTP-Hangzhou board meeting approved a resolution to remove Han as CTP-Hangzhou's corporate representative on Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd and to replace him with Patrick.

On 21 October 2010, company secretary Ng Chee Tiong emailed Han the resolutions recording his removal from his positions in CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd; the resolutions did not state reasons for the removal.

On 29 October 2010, after Han's removal, Michael Heng emailed Patrick Teo and Lim Shih Hsi Richard proposing an action agenda for himself and Patrick regarding the Hangzhou project and stating that Han had not carried out the listed management activities, and that the new agenda would support Han's removal as chairman of CTP-Hangzhou and from Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd together with Robin.
On 30 November 2010, Vanwarm chairman Liu Hai travelled to Singapore to mediate between Han and the other parties involved in the Hangzhou project.

On 2 December 2010, during discussions with Liu Hai in Singapore, the defendants indicated agreement in principle to reinstate Han and Christine to the CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board in line with the 1 March 2010 document, while Han requested an additional assurance that he would not be removed again until after completion of the Hangzhou project.
On 3 December 2010, Han commenced legal proceedings against the defendants by serving a writ of summons.

On 31 January 2011, Hangzhou Qianjiang Economic Development Area Management Committee issued a Letter of Notice to Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and Vanwarm stating that Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd's registered capital had not been injected by the agreed deadline of 31 December 2010 and that construction work had not started by the end of 2010, and warning that if the capital was not injected by an extended deadline of 10 March 2011, the tripartite agreement would be terminated.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the 1 March 2010 document and the parties' pre-dispute conduct, Han Cheng Fong had a contractual or agreed right to remain as chairman and director of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and whether his subsequent removal from those positions on 12 October 2010 corresponded to a dismissal outcome.
Party_Details.Rule: In company and contract law, the effect of a written agreement or resolution among shareholders and participants is assessed objectively by examining its terms and the surrounding communications to determine whether it constitutes a binding agreement on shareholding, board composition and decision-making, including any requirement for unanimous consent to alter specified arrangements. A director or office-holder who has an agreed right to hold a position may only be removed consistently with the terms of that agreement and applicable company law procedures; otherwise, removal may be treated as a dismissal or termination of that agreed position.
Party_Details.Application: The 1 March 2010 document, together with contemporaneous emails in February and March 2010, recorded specific share allocations in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, the creation and ownership of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, and Han's designation as chairman of CTP-Hangzhou's board, with a clause requiring unanimous board consent to change the recorded resolutions. Subsequent conduct, including Han's appointment as chairman of CTP-Hangzhou upon its incorporation on 1 April 2010 and his role in signing the collaboration agreement with Vanwarm on 31 May 2010, aligned with these recorded arrangements. On 12 October 2010, an Extraordinary General Meeting and board meeting of CTP-Hangzhou removed Han from his directorship and from his role as corporate representative on the joint venture without prior notice to him and without recorded reasons, and without evidence of unanimous consent among all parties who had signed the 1 March 2010 document. These pre-dispute events frame the later judicial determination of whether the removal complied with or departed from the agreed framework.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analysed whether the 1 March 2010 document was a binding shareholders' agreement, considering its language, the parties' emails, and the fact that non-directors signed it. The judge rejected the defendants' characterisation of the document as a mere board resolution and found it to be a valid shareholders' agreement that conferred on Han a right to be and remain chairman and director of CTPHZ, subject to unanimous consent for changes. The court also accepted that Han had a legitimate expectation, based on the circumstances of his joining the project, that he would continue in those roles. The judge examined extensive email evidence showing that Patrick and Richard planned to dilute Han and Christine, freeze them out of information, and remove them, including through proposed share sales to BKCB and a 'covert' dilution scheme. The court treated these as evidence of a conspiracy by unlawful means. The defendants advanced multiple justifications for Han's removal, including alleged failure to raise funds, decisions on the RESOHO component, an alleged attempt to seize control of CTPHZ on 29 September 2010, failure to meet project deadlines, exclusion of Michael from the CTPHZ board, failure to perform management tasks, competition via other projects, and disclosure of internal disputes to Vanwarm. The judge evaluated each ground, often relying on cross-examination admissions, and found them unproven or inconsistent, noting that decisions such as non-participation in the RESOHO component were collective and that Patrick himself accepted responsibility for missed deadlines and for Michael's non-appointment. On the disclosure issue, the court distinguished Tokuhon, holding that Han's communications to Vanwarm about internal disputes and the proposed Malaysian share sale did not cause the loss of the Hangzhou project and occurred in a context where Patrick and Richard were themselves pursuing a sale inconsistent with the China-Singapore understanding. The court concluded that the defendants had wrongfully dismissed Han on 12 October 2010 in breach of the shareholders' agreement and his legitimate expectations, that Patrick and Richard had conspired by unlawful means to injure Han, and that Han was entitled to damages to be assessed. The court also ordered that CTP and CTPHZ be wound up under section 216 of the Companies Act and awarded costs to Han.

Party_Details.Name: Teo Chong Nghee Patrick

Facts.Text: Patrick Teo Chong Nghee is a founder director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, a Singapore company involved in the Hangzhou-Singapore Eco-Park project.

As at early 2010, Patrick Teo was a director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and a director of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd after its incorporation, and also a director of Cleantech Partners Hangzhou Pte Ltd's parent company.
Patrick Teo and Lim Shih Hsi Richard controlled Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd, which was designated as manager and shareholder of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd under the 1 March 2010 document.

On 1 March 2010, Patrick Teo signed the 1 March document recording share allocations in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, the establishment of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd as a wholly-owned subsidiary, and the appointment of Han as Deputy Chairman of CTP and Chairman of CTP-Hangzhou.

On 23 March 2010, as a director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, Patrick was involved in CTP entering into a tripartite agreement with Hangzhou Qianjiang Economic Development Area Management Committee and Vanwarm for the Hangzhou-Singapore Eco-Park project.

On 30 January 2010, Patrick emailed the other directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd stating that CTP lacked real estate track record, political connections and savvy in China, and that Han had the prominence, aura and personality needed to represent CTP to Vanwarm.

On 24 March 2010, following an interview with Patrick, Lim Shih Hsi Richard and Michael Heng, the Business Times reported that Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd was exploring multiple energy and clean-tech projects in Indonesia, Vietnam and China.

On 7 April 2010, Patrick and the other directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd accepted Han's decision communicated by email to focus on CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and not sit on the CTP board to avoid affecting CTP's fund-raising.

On 31 May 2010, Patrick, as part of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd's leadership, allowed Han to sign a collaboration agreement with Vanwarm under which CTP-Hangzhou would receive a guaranteed profit of RMB130 million and a USD6 million loan from Hong Jia to fund its 40% share of the joint venture's registered capital.

On 3 June 2010, Patrick received an email from Lim Shih Hsi Richard proposing to reduce Han's share to 10%, remove him from the chairmanship of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, remove Han and Christine as bank signatories, and freeze them from information until they felt useless.

On 3 June 2010, Patrick replied to Richard stating that achieving the goals regarding Han and Christine would trigger confrontation, that he preferred an 'elegant' approach via a marathon meeting, that CTP and CTP-Hangzhou were cleantech vendors so Han and Christine's roles could be diminished, and that they could freeze Han and Christine from further information and updates until they felt useless, anticipating that Han might resign.
On 3 June 2010, in response to Patrick's email about Han possibly resigning, Richard replied that this outcome was their plan.

On 1 April 2010, CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd was incorporated as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, and Patrick was appointed as one of its directors alongside Han, Robin, Richard and Christine.

On 17 June 2010, Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd was incorporated in China, and CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd appointed Han and Robin as its directors; Patrick remained involved through CTP-Hangzhou's shareholding.

By August 2010, Patrick and Richard were exploring a sale of their shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd to Bintan Kindenko Corporation Berhad, a Malaysian company, via intermediary Frankie Tan and BKCB's executive chairman Ong Puay Koon.
On 17 August 2010, Frankie Tan emailed Patrick suggesting that Han and Christine should be removed before concluding a deal with BKCB because they might block the deal once announced.

On 17 August 2010, Patrick emailed Frankie describing a plan involving a Malaysian company controlled by Frankie and Ong Puay Koon to subscribe for shares so that Han and Christine's combined shareholding could be diluted to about 19.23% or further, and stating that at the time of the cash subscription they would ask Han to step down from the chair of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and Christine from Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.
On 24 August 2010, Patrick emailed Richard and Robin stating that he was planning something with Frankie to get rid of Han and Christine and that they were going to be ruthless.

On 30 August 2010, Patrick emailed lawyer Harpal Mahtani stating that he and his partners planned to exchange their shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd for shares in a public listed company, that an altercation with Han would create problems, and that he needed to get Han out of the way or neutralise him.
On 5 September 2010, Patrick emailed the other CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd directors stating that he would address issues raised by Han in a memo to Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd members at a later date.

On 27 September 2010, Patrick emailed Richard and Robin asking them not to attend the 29 September 2010 CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board meeting, stating that attendance could lead to a breakup of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and that Han had planned to catch them off guard.

On 1 October 2010, Patrick instructed company secretary Ng Chee Tiong to retain CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd's records until further notice because the majority of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd's board did not agree to the change of company secretary decided at the 29 September 2010 CTP-Hangzhou board meeting.
On 6 October 2010, Patrick emailed Han stating that he did not have the time or passion to spar with Han and reserving his comments on Han's views on corporate governance.

On 12 October 2010, Patrick arranged for an Extraordinary General Meeting of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd at which Han and Christine were removed as directors with immediate effect and Michael was appointed a director, and on the same day participated in a CTP-Hangzhou board meeting that resolved to remove Han as CTP-Hangzhou's corporate representative on Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd and to appoint Patrick in his place.

On 21 October 2010, company secretary Ng Chee Tiong emailed Han the resolutions recording his removal from his positions in CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd; the resolutions did not state reasons for the removal.

On 28 October 2010, Patrick and Michael travelled to Hangzhou to meet Vanwarm chairman Liu Hai, during which Liu Hai requested that Han and Robin remain as directors of Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd while the company was bidding for land, and Patrick agreed.

On 29 October 2010, Patrick received an email from Michael proposing an action agenda for himself and Patrick regarding the Hangzhou project and stating that Han had not carried out the listed management activities, and that the new agenda would support Han's removal as chairman of CTP-Hangzhou and from Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd together with Robin.

On 2 December 2010, during mediation efforts by Liu Hai in Singapore, Patrick and the other defendants indicated agreement in principle to reinstate Han and Christine to the CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd board in line with the 1 March 2010 document, further conditions.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of the 1 March 2010 document and subsequent pre-dispute communications and corporate actions, Patrick Teo and the other defendants' removal of Han from his positions in CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and related steps in relation to the Hangzhou project corresponded to a finding of dismissal and conspiracy by unlawful means.
Party_Details.Rule: A director or shareholder who participates in a binding agreement governing shareholdings, board composition and decision-making may not unilaterally depart from that agreement when effecting changes to board positions or corporate control. A combination of persons who agree to use unlawful means, such as acting in of a binding agreement or company law requirements, to cause loss to another may be found to have engaged in a conspiracy by unlawful means when their plan is implemented and causes the intended harm.
Party_Details.Application: Patrick signed the 1 March 2010 document that set out share allocations in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, the establishment and board composition of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, and a unanimity requirement for changes to the recorded resolutions. Subsequent emails in June, August and September 2010 show Patrick and Richard discussing reducing Han's economic participation, removing him from the chairmanship of CTP-Hangzhou, freezing him and Christine out of information, and using share transactions with a Malaysian listed company to dilute their holdings and secure Han's resignation or removal. On 12 October 2010, Patrick initiated and participated in an Extraordinary General Meeting and board meeting of CTP-Hangzhou that removed Han and Christine as directors and replaced Han as corporate representative on the joint venture, without prior notice to Han and without recorded reasons, and without evidence of unanimous consent among all signatories to the 1 March 2010 document. These steps, together with the pre-dispute communications, formed the factual basis for the court's later conclusions on dismissal and conspiracy.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined Patrick's role as a founder director of CTP and CTPHZ and as a signatory to the 1 March 2010 document. It rejected his characterisation of the document as a non-binding internal resolution, noting that non-directors signed it and that its unanimity clause functioned as a voting agreement among shareholders. The judge relied on Patrick's cross-examination admissions that there was an agreement to give Han shares and to implement the profit-sharing clauses. The court analysed numerous emails in which Patrick and Richard discussed reducing Han's share, removing him from the chairmanship, freezing him and Christine out of information, and using a 'covert' dilution scheme involving a Malaysian company to dilute their holdings and secure their removal. The judge treated these as clear evidence of a plan to use unlawful means to injure Han. The court found that Patrick orchestrated the 12 October 2010 EGM and board meeting that removed Han and Christine without notice and without justification consistent with the shareholders' agreement. It evaluated each justification advanced by Patrick, including alleged failures by Han regarding fundraising, the RESOHO component, management tasks, deadlines, and competition, and found them unsupported or contradicted by Patrick's own admissions. The court concluded that Patrick and Richard had conspired by unlawful means to remove Han and that the dismissal was wrongful, leading to liability for damages and to orders winding up CTP and CTPHZ.

Party_Details.Name: Lim Shih Hsi Richard

Facts.Text: Lim Shih Hsi Richard is a founder director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and a director of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd after its incorporation.
Richard Lim and Patrick Teo controlled Cleantech Ventures Asia Pte Ltd, which was designated as manager and shareholder of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd under the 1 March 2010 document.

On 23 February 2010, Richard emailed Christine and others confirming that Christine had proposed a formal shareholders' agreement among Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd shareholders and stating that they would prepare a first draft soon.

On 28 February 2010, Richard emailed that Christine had requested a simple shareholders' agreement among the six directors of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd to be signed by 1 March 2010 and that he had prepared a resolution to be sent out by 1 March covering shareholdings, appointments of Han and Christine as directors, Han as Vice Chairman of CTP and its subsidiaries, and profit sharing.

On 1 March 2010, Richard signed the 1 March document recording share allocations in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, the establishment of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, and the appointment of Han as Deputy Chairman of CTP and Chairman of CTP-Hangzhou.

On 1 April 2010, CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd was incorporated as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd, and Richard was appointed as one of its directors alongside Han, Patrick, Robin and Christine.

On 11 March 2010, Richard emailed Patrick, Robin and Christine stating that he would tell Michael that a planned meeting was postponed so that the others could meet without Michael to discuss sensitive issues including the RMB130 million guaranteed profit for Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.
On 1 April 2010, Richard emailed Vanwarm stating that Michael should not be informed about the source of funds for Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd's participation in the Hangzhou project.

On 3 June 2010, Richard emailed Patrick proposing to reduce Han's share to a maximum of 10%, remove him from the chairmanship of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, remove Han and Christine as bank signatories, and freeze them from further information and updates until they felt useless, and stating that they should not leave 'luggage' behind.
On 3 June 2010, after Patrick suggested that Han might threaten to resign and that Christine might depart, Richard replied that this was their plan.

By August 2010, Richard and Patrick were exploring a sale of their shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd to Bintan Kindenko Corporation Berhad, a Malaysian company, via intermediary Frankie Tan and BKCB's executive chairman Ong Puay Koon.
On 24 August 2010, Richard received an email from Patrick stating that Patrick was planning something with Frankie to get rid of Han and Christine and that they were going to be ruthless.

On 3 June 2010, Richard emailed Patrick objecting strongly to including Michael as a shareholder in another company, stating that he and his associates '1 million percent' objected and that they did not need Michael.

On 1 April 2010, Richard liaised with the company secretary regarding the incorporation of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and the appointment of its initial directors, resulting in Han, Patrick, Robin and Christine being appointed, with one board seat left vacant.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute agreements and communications, Lim Shih Hsi Richard participated with others in a plan to remove Han from his positions in CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd in a manner later characterised as conspiracy by unlawful means and dismissal.
Party_Details.Rule: Where multiple individuals coordinate to implement steps that depart from a binding agreement on shareholding and governance, and those steps are directed at removing another participant from agreed positions, a court may treat their coordinated conduct as a conspiracy by unlawful means if it involves of contractual or company law constraints and results in the intended harm.
Party_Details.Application: Richard was a signatory to the 1 March 2010 document that set out share allocations, the creation and board composition of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, and a unanimity requirement for changes. His emails in February 2010 he understood the document as implementing a shareholders' agreement requested by Christine on Han's behalf. Subsequent emails in March and June 2010 show Richard proposing to reduce Han's share, remove him from the chairmanship of CTP-Hangzhou, remove Han and Christine as bank signatories, and freeze them from information until they felt useless, and agreeing with Patrick that inducing Han's resignation was their plan. By August 2010, Richard was involved with Patrick in exploring a share sale to a Malaysian company that would alter the ownership structure. These pre-dispute communications and his role in the corporate structure formed the factual basis for the court's later finding that he participated in a conspiracy by unlawful means and in the removal of Han.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Richard's role in drafting and circulating the 1 March 2010 document and his emails describing it as a simple shareholders' agreement requested by Christine. It found that his explanations for why Han, Christine and CTVA signed the document were unconvincing and that the document functioned as a binding shareholders' agreement. The judge examined Richard's emails proposing to reduce Han's share, remove him from the chairmanship, strip him and Christine of signing authority, and freeze them out of information, and his agreement with Patrick that inducing Han's resignation was their plan. The court also noted Richard's involvement in excluding Michael from certain discussions and in keeping Michael uninformed about funding sources. In cross-examination, Richard admitted that the proposed share sale plans were intended to remove Han and that the plans would result in Han's removal from CTPHZ's board. The judge found his evidence inconsistent, particularly regarding Michael's non-appointment to the CTPHZ board, and concluded that Richard's credibility was severely undermined. On this basis, the court held that Richard, together with Patrick, had conspired by unlawful means to remove Han and that their actions contributed to Han's wrongful dismissal.

Party_Details.Name: Michael Heng Swee Hai

Facts.Text: Michael Heng Swee Hai is a founder director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd.
As at early 2010, Michael was a director of Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd but was not initially appointed to the board of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 1 April 2010.

On 1 March 2010, Michael signed the 1 March document recording that he would hold two ordinary shares in Cleantech Partners Pte Ltd and that he would be one of six directors of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd when formed.

On 10 February 2010, Michael emailed Patrick expressing that he preferred to be seen as a key driver in the Hangzhou project and that in the proposed setup his presence in the project appeared to have disappeared.

On 1 April 2010, CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd was incorporated with Han, Patrick, Richard, Robin and Christine as directors, and one board seat left vacant; Michael was not appointed to the CTP-Hangzhou board at that time.

On 11 March 2010, Michael was informed by Richard that a planned meeting was postponed, while Richard separately informed others that the postponement was to allow a meeting without Michael to discuss sensitive issues.
On 12 October 2010, at an Extraordinary General Meeting of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd, Michael was appointed as a director of CTP-Hangzhou on the same day that Han and Christine were removed as directors.

On 28 October 2010, Michael travelled with Patrick to Hangzhou to meet Vanwarm chairman Liu Hai to discuss matters relating to the Hangzhou project and the composition of the Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd board.

On 29 October 2010, Michael emailed Patrick and Richard proposing an action agenda for himself and Patrick regarding the Hangzhou project, listing various management tasks and stating that Han had not carried out those tasks, and that the new agenda would support Han's removal as chairman of CTP-Hangzhou and from Hangzhou Vanwarm Cleantech Co Ltd together with Robin.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, on the pre-dispute record, Michael Heng's involvement in the governance of CTP-Hangzhou Pte Ltd and his communications about Han's role contributed to the outcome on liability for dismissal and conspiracy.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who joins a board after key agreements are in place may still be implicated in removal of another director if he participates in or endorses steps that implement an unlawful plan, but liability depends on the extent of his knowledge and participation in the underlying agreement and scheme.
Party_Details.Application: Michael was a signatory to the 1 March 2010 document and a founder director of CTP, but he was not initially appointed to the CTP-Hangzhou board when it was incorporated on 1 April 2010. Pre-dispute emails other directors sometimes excluded him from sensitive discussions about the Hangzhou project and funding. On 12 October 2010, he was appointed to the CTP-Hangzhou board at the same meeting where Han and Christine were removed, and on 29 October 2010 he sent an email proposing an action agenda and asserting that Han had not performed certain management tasks, suggesting that this would support Han's removal. These facts were considered by the court in assessing his role relative to Patrick and Richard.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court noted that Michael was a founder director of CTP and a signatory to the 1 March 2010 document, but that he was not initially appointed to the CTPHZ board. Evidence showed that Patrick and Richard sometimes kept him out of sensitive meetings and information. The judge considered Michael's complaint email of 10 February 2010 about his diminished role and his later email of 29 October 2010 proposing an action agenda and asserting that Han had not performed management tasks, which the defendants later relied on as a justification for Han's removal. While the primary focus of the conspiracy finding was on Patrick and Richard, the court treated Michael as part of the group of defendants responsible for Han's wrongful dismissal and for the corporate actions taken after Han's removal, and entered judgment against all defendants accordingly.
